channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: New Winged Robot Can Fly and Swim Like a Puffin
item.description: Inspired by the physical feats of diving birds, researchers have created a robot that can plunge into the water and flap back into the air.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway

item.title: Researchers Claim to Have Solved the Perplexing ‘Reverse Sprinkler’ Problem That Stumped Feynman
item.description: Physicists have debated which way a submerged sprinkler sucking in water would spin. Careful experiments provide an answer.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang

item.title: A Sweet Surprise: Scientists Find Sugar Deep in Our Galaxy
item.description: It’s the first time a sugar molecule has been detected in interstellar space. The discovery provides tantalizing new clues into how life may have arisen on Earth.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Dzombak
item.media:description: The galactic center of the Milky Way, captured by the Spitzer Space Telescope in 2006.

item.title: Dark Sky Defenders Raise Alarm Along the Border of West Texas
item.description: Residents fear that the Trump administration’s plans to build a border wall will threaten the region’s spectacular starry skies.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller and Emily Elconin
item.media:credit: Emily Elconin for The New York Times

item.title: ‘We’re Fighting Satan’: The War to Save Bees From a Hornet Invasion
item.description: As yellow-legged hornets spread through South Carolina’s Lowcountry region, threatening the local honey crop, a state team of bee defenders is racing to the rescue.
item.dc:creator: Jack Tamisiea and Sean Rayford
item.media:credit: Sean Rayford for The New York Times

item.title: What Is an Aortic Dissection?
item.description: The condition that killed Senator Lindsey Graham is deadly, sudden and difficult to treat — “like a knife to the heart,” one expert said.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Lindsey Graham speaking to reporters on Capitol Hill last year. He died on Saturday.

item.title: F.C.C. Approves Test of Space Mirror to Light Night Sky Despite Outcry
item.description: A start-up company has permission to try its plan to bounce solar rays onto the dark side of Earth, turning night to day for a three-mile-wide patch.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:description: An artist’s concept of space mirrors in orbit.

item.title: What China’s Successful Rocket Launch Means for the Future of the Space Race
item.description: A space neophyte not long ago, China is now the United States’s main competitor for supremacy throughout the solar system.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: An image released by the Chinese state-run Xinhua News Agency purporting to show the returning first stage of the Long March 10B rocket on a seaborne platform in the South China Sea on Friday.

item.title: Your Cat Is Being Nice? Think Again.
item.description: A new study finds that sometimes cats groom each other specifically to be annoying.
item.dc:creator: Taylor Mitchell Brown

item.title: Climate: An extraordinary White House meeting
item.description: A new book sheds light on President Trump’s affinity for fossil fuels.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: An oil refinery in Punto Fijo, Venezuela. In a meeting with the president, the Chevron chief executive pushed for an extension of the company’s license to operate in the country.

item.title: Public Health Groups Sue F.D.A. Over Flavored E-Cigarette Policy
item.description: Executives unhappy with the agency donated and lobbied President Trump directly. Major companies have already begun to prepare and ship new products.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:description: Flavored electronic cigarette devices displayed for sale at a store in Pinecrest, Fla. in 2023.

item.title: Roger Worthington Is Suing Over a Heat Wave
item.description: Roger Worthington is helping to lead a $50 billion lawsuit against oil producers. This week he’ll face powerful critics in Congress.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Michael Hanson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Roger Worthington in a garden adjacent to his brewery in Bend, Ore.

item.title: Trump Sharply Cuts the Size of Two National Monuments in Utah
item.description: Native American tribes and environmental groups are expected to challenge the move to shrink the Bears Ears and Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monuments.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:description: A hiker in the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument in Utah.

item.title: Appeals Court Revives Lawsuits Tying Tylenol Use in Pregnancy to Autism and A.D.H.D.
item.description: A federal appeals court overruled a district court judge who dismissed the lawsuits, citing unreliable scientific evidence.
item.dc:creator: Azeen Ghorayshi
item.media:description: Major medical groups have consistently stressed that Tylenol is the safest option to treat pain or fever during pregnancy.

item.title: Years After He Quit Smoking, a Lung Cancer Scan Saved His Life
item.description: Lung cancer remains the leading cause of cancer deaths in the United States and older adults are at higher risk. But only about a quarter of patients eligible for screening are up-to-date.
item.dc:creator: Paula Span
item.category: United States Preventive Services Task Force

item.title: Kids Can’t Stop Watching ‘Moana.’ There’s a Scientific Explanation.
item.description: The 2016 release has become the most watched movie on Disney+. Parents and experts explain why kids can’t get enough.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:description: Streams of “Moana” on Disney+ total more than 1.5 billion hours.

item.title: Trump Cuts Habitat Protections for Endangered Species
item.description: The rule change ends a safeguard that had been in place for 50 years and could hasten the demise of imperiled animals.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn and Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Piping plovers need undisturbed beaches to nest and raise their young.

item.title: U.S. Department of Energy Underestimated Potential Los Alamos Plutonium Leak Danger, Study Finds
item.description: An accident at the lab that produces America’s nuclear bomb cores could lead to more fatalities than previously estimated by the federal government, according to new research.
item.dc:creator: Alicia Inez Guzmán
item.media:credit: Nina Riggio for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Los Alamos National Laboratory in New Mexico is home to the country’s only active plutonium-handling facility.

item.title: She Studied Ways to Make People Smarter. Then Her Grant Was Cut.
item.description: Jessica Cantlon had a grant with the U.S. Navy to explore whether people could improve spatial problem-solving skills through training. Then the program was cut.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Jake Reinhart for The New York Times

item.title: New York Sues Companies Over ‘Forever Chemicals’
item.description: The lawsuit claims that the companies, including 3M and DuPont, contaminated the environment and engaged in fraud by withholding information from the public about the harms of the chemicals, known as PFAS.
item.dc:creator: Christina Caron and Hilary Howard
item.media:description: Letitia James, the New York attorney general, said that chemical companies operating in the state “knowingly sold toxic products.”

item.title: Nobel-Winning U.S. Chemist Omar Yaghi Will Move to China to Lead A.I. Institute
item.description: Omar Yaghi of the University of California, Berkeley, will head an initiative to apply artificial intelligence to the discovery of new materials.
item.dc:creator: William J. Broad
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Omar Yaghi shared last year’s Nobel Prize in Chemistry for helping develop frameworks of chemical building blocks with vast internal surface areas, which he named metal-organic frameworks.

item.title: Advanced Recycling Hits a Rough Patch
item.description: In the past weeks, two sites in the United States have halted operations.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Unlike traditional mechanical recycling, advanced recycling can theoretically process mixed or contaminated plastics that otherwise would end up in landfills.

item.title: Joseph Fraumeni, 93, Dies; Helped Discover Genetic Link to Cancer
item.description: Considered the founder of molecular epidemiology, he worked with Frederick P. Li to identify a hereditary disorder that increased the risk of developing cancer at a young age.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel

item.title: This Star Just Ate a Planet, and It’s Not Done Yet
item.description: A star 1,300 light-years away appears to have just consumed one world and is gearing up for seconds.
item.dc:creator: Becky Ferreira

item.title: Trump’s Plans for Nuclear Power Lurch Ahead
item.description: Recent technological advances have brought the president’s goals a bit closer to reality.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:description: Energy Secretary Chris Wright, center, visited the National Reactor Innovation Center near Idaho Falls, Idaho, last month.

item.title: Wally Funk, Who Set an Age Record for Space Travel, Dies at 87
item.description: As a young woman in the 1960s, she wasn’t allowed to become a NASA astronaut. She finally realized her long-held dream of flying in space as an octogenarian.
item.dc:creator: Richard Goldstein
item.media:description: Wally Funk in 2021, after returning from a roughly 10-minute spaceflight aboard a rocket built by the company Blue Origin. “I loved every minute of it,” she said. “I just wish it had been longer.”

item.title: These Fossils May Be the Earliest Evidence of Handedness in Animals
item.description: Scientists propose that recently uncovered fossils may be the earliest evidence of behavioral “handedness” in animals.
item.dc:creator: Jack Tamisiea

item.title: A Chinese Spacecraft Captures First Image of Quasi-Moon
item.description: A Chinese spacecraft has captured the first image of the asteroid Kamo‘oalewa.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews

item.title: I.U.C.N. Update Says Deep-Sea Mining Threatens Mollusks Around Hydrothermal Vents
item.description: The snails and other mollusks around hydrothermal vents have evolved to thrive in extreme conditions, but mineral extraction could drive more than half to extinction.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.category: International Union for Conservation of Nature

item.title: How to Turn Your Phone Into a Personal Health Dashboard
item.description: Free apps from Google, Samsung and Apple can help you track your diet, exercise and well-being — and provide vital information during emergencies.
item.dc:creator: J. D. Biersdorfer
item.media:description: From left, the Google Health, the Samsung Health and Apple’s Health apps offer plenty of tools and features to help you manage your diet, exercise and personal safety.

item.title: This Kind of Obsessive Attraction Isn’t Love. But It Has a Name.
item.description: Limerence is more than a crush, psychologists say, and it can persist for months or years.
item.dc:creator: Melinda Wenner Moyer

item.title: Western Europe Had Its Hottest June on Record
item.description: An unusually early heat wave smashed records across France, Britain and Spain. Temperatures in some areas are starting to soar again.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:description: Riding a hot train in London on June 24. Average temperatures across Western Europe last month were 3.05 degrees Celsius, or 5.5 degrees Fahrenheit, above the mean from recent decades.

item.title: Spread of Seafloor Directly Observed for the First Time
item.description: The spread of the ocean floor, as tectonic plates spread apart, is known but hard to observe. Scientists have now documented the process in action.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway
item.media:description: Île Saint-Paul, a tiny island in the Indian Ocean, some 120 miles southwest of the deep sea site where the Antarctica and Australia tectonic plates diverged during an earthquake in 2024.

item.title: Global Warming Could Ravage the Amazon’s Useful Plant Species, Study Finds
item.description: Hotter temperatures and harsher droughts could cause Indigenous societies to lose many of the species they have used for medicine, rituals and more, scientists found.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong and Flávia Milhorance
item.media:credit: Victor Moriyama for The New York Times
item.media:description: The study warned that major losses of plant species could occur in the coming decades even if nations limit global warming to moderate levels.

item.title: Our Bacteria Are Talking. We’ve Just Begun to Understand What They’re Saying.
item.description: The human microbiome is essential to our health, but scientists still know very little about it. Two researchers set out to map this largely uncharted terrain.
item.dc:creator: Jeneen Interlandi

item.title: Chernobyl’s Wildlife Cameras Reveal How War Affects Wild Animals
item.description: A rare camera-trap study logged the effects of armed conflict on wild animals in real time.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:credit: Frankfurt Zoological Society, Chernobyl Radiation and Ecological Biosphere Reserve/Wild Polissia Program
item.media:description: Researchers found few behavioral changes in lynxes, whereas different species of deer changed their activity patterns during the Russian occupation.
